attractors.label: A1
nearest_texts: ProtoFair: Fair Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning via Pseudo-Counterfactual Pairs attacks the bottleneck that SSL encoders can learn high-quality visual features while silently encoding demographic signal, causing equalized-odds violations downstream on face attributes even when no target labels are used. Rather than following the dominant fairness workflow of redesigning the SSL objective, adding adversarial heads, or requiring supervised positives, it pivots from loss replacement toward a plug-in regularizer that leaves SimCLR/SupCon intact and only asks for sensitive-attribute annotations. The mechanical shift is to repurpose unsupervised prototype clustering as a proxy for semantic content: a separate cluster head maps features into a prototype space, prototypes are initialized by K-means after warmup and tracked by EMA, and samples with the same cluster assignment but different sensitive groups are treated as pseudo-counterfactual positives. ProtoFair then pulls these cross-group, content-matched pairs together while excluding same-cluster/same-group pairs from the numerator, and extends the signal with a FIFO cross-batch queue so rare pairs can be discovered beyond a mini-batch. This is unusual because clustering, usually used to improve representation quality, becomes the fairness mechanism itself and is detached to avoid degenerate cluster collapse. On CelebA and UTKFace with ResNet-18 and linear evaluation, ProtoFair consistently lowers equalized odds across imbalance regimes while keeping competitive accuracy, with especially strong gains under demographic skew. The paper points toward modular fairness controls that can ride on future SSL backbones without retraining the whole objective.

Graph Federated Unlearning for Privacy Preservation tackles a gap in graph federated learning: even when raw user graphs stay local, withdrawn users’ signals can persist in shared parameters and continue leaking through membership inference or inversion after “client removal,” violating the right to be forgotten. The paper pivots away from the common assumption that federated privacy is satisfied once data never leaves the client, and away from brute-force retraining or generic machine-unlearning recipes that treat graph clients like iid records. Instead it moves from simply erasing a client toward erasing its information while keeping the graph system usable. The core mechanical shift is FedGCV, which combines gradient correction with virtual clients: unlearning updates are projected to stay orthogonal to the gradients of retained data, so forgetting a target client minimally disturbs other clients’ optimization, and the server synthesizes privacy-preserving proxy subgraphs with a VGAE, Laplacian spectral constraints, and feature sampling to replace removed clients without reconstructing their raw entities. This is unusual among nearby work because it repairs topology and embedding continuity explicitly, rather than only adding noise or ascent steps. Evaluated on Cora, CiteSeer, PubMed, and Tolokers under a user-withdrawal RTBF setting, it reports lower MIA rates and higher post-unlearning accuracy than seven baselines, sometimes even outperforming retraining on utility. The limitation is that it relies on controlled synthetic client generation and federated server orchestration, pointing toward future graph privacy systems that jointly model unlearning, topology-preserving replacement, and attack-aware evaluation.

Adversarial Update-Based Federated Unlearning for Poisoned Model Recovery attacks the post-detection problem in federated learning where malicious client updates have already contaminated the global model, so simply identifying and removing attackers is not enough; the model must be repaired without paying the full cost of retraining from scratch. The paper explicitly challenges the older recovery story that unlearning is a form of numerical rollback or complete-history replay, which either demands impractical storage or leaves residual poisoning. It pivots from reconstructing the training trajectory toward synthesizing counteracting updates: FAUN keeps only a short window of recent malicious updates, averages them into a representative harmful direction, then uses projected-gradient adversarial optimization on a small clean proxy dataset to amplify the worst-case effect of those updates and turn them into an elimination signal. The mechanical shift is to subtract these adversarially derived updates for only a few unlearning rounds, then hand control back to ordinary benign fine-tuning, so recovery is driven by a min-max elimination step rather than full restart. This is unusual because the server is not trying to approximate past training, but to actively push the poisoned model away from malicious directions while preserving benign progress. On MNIST, CIFAR-10, and AG News under IID and non-IID settings, with CNN, ResNet-18, and FastText models, FAUN matches retraining-level recovery, drives backdoor ASR near zero, and does so in far fewer rounds than FedRecover, FedEraser, or Crab. The work points toward lightweight, adversarially controlled unlearning as a general recovery primitive for federated security.

Robust and Explainable Divide-and-Conquer Learning for Intrusion Detection attacks the bottleneck that payload-based intrusion detectors face in high-dimensional, noisy, class-imbalanced traffic: global models are expensive, brittle under adversarial perturbation, and opaque to analysts on memory-limited devices. The paper challenges the usual assumption that one should either scale up a monolithic classifier or impose a generic modular hierarchy; instead it pivots from global fitting and blind mixtures toward correlation-aware decomposition of the label space. The mechanical shift is SCAL: it builds class fingerprints from per-class compression code lengths on raw payloads, turns those fingerprints into a class-correlation matrix, then uses hierarchical clustering to group positively correlated attack types into subproblems. A learned instance distributor routes flows to local decision-tree classifiers, and SCAL explicitly falls back to a single learning problem when decomposition yields no benefit, making the partitioning conditional rather than mandatory. This is unusual relative to mixtures of experts or binary hierarchical classifiers because the split is driven by local traffic similarity and evaluated jointly on accuracy, cost, robustness, and explainability. On UNSW-NB15, CIC-IDS2017, IoT, and VNAT payload benchmarks, SCAL improves minority-class local F1 by up to 43.3%, shrinks model size by up to 257×, and remains as or more resilient than global baselines under Cube attack, while decision-tree pruning can compress local models into human-auditable rules. The work points toward adaptive, multi-objective security analytics where modularity is learned from data geometry rather than imposed by architecture.

DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning attacks the deployment gap in unlearning: models can pass BF16 privacy audits yet resurrect forgotten data after the routine move to low-bit inference. The paper pivots the field from treating forgetting as a static BF16 training outcome toward treating it as a precision-dependent property that must survive INT4 deployment, challenging the implicit assumption that unlearning metrics are invariant to quantization. The mechanical shift is adapter-space INT4 perturbation in the NF4+LoRA regime, evaluated on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU for seven unlearning methods; the authors introduce Q-INT4 as the missing robustness metric and show INT8 is benign while INT4 can amplify recovery by up to 22×, with harder datasets worsening the effect. Nearby unlearning work is differentiated by its focus on the LoRA adapter weight space rather than merged-weight PTQ, and by the discovery of an FA–RA–Q-INT4 trilemma: no baseline simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, preserved retain accuracy, and quantization robustness. To counter this, DURABLEUN-SAF adds a quantization-aware forgetting loss, backpropagating through INT4 rounding with a Straight-Through Estimator and warmup, effectively flattening the forget landscape under quantization. The result is a sharp phase transition: once robustness appears, retain accuracy collapses and cannot be tuned back. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method to obtain a stable 3/3 empirical durability certificate, pointing toward quantization-aware unlearning protocols, precision-adaptive certificates, and future selective robustness control in deployment-native privacy evaluation.
attractors.explanation:
Attractor A1 Explanation:

This basin is about making models safe to use after training by removing what should no longer be remembered and preventing sensitive information from leaking back out. The shared pattern is not general robustness, but controlled erasure: papers here study federated unlearning, poisoned-model repair, and related methods that can subtract a user, a client, or a bad update without retraining everything from scratch. What distinguishes it is that the key question is lifecycle integrity—can the model truly forget, stay private, and still work after deployment changes such as compression or low-bit inference? That makes it different from neighboring reliability work that mainly improves prediction quality or perception. A paper like Graph Federated Unlearning focuses on the right to be forgotten in distributed training, while DurableUn shows that forgetting can fail again once the model is quantized for deployment.

attractors.label: A2
nearest_texts: SCPRM: A Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model for Knowledge Graph Question Answering attacks the failure mode where process reward models “rescue” a risky reasoning path by averaging early mistakes with later correct hops, a flaw that becomes acute in KGQA because only a few multi-hop paths are logically valid and one wrong edge can invalidate the whole derivation, especially in medical and legal queries where false-but-plausible paths are dangerous. The paper pivots from additive, step-independent PRM scoring and outcome-only verification toward prefix-conditioned, risk-sensitive trajectory evaluation: the mechanical shift is an A*-style reward decomposition that replaces next-token-style scoring with cumulative past reward plus schema-aware future reward. SCPRM models past safety multiplicatively over the reasoning prefix, then adds a learned future head that estimates how far the current path’s inferred schema is from the query’s implicit target schema, parsed from the question and represented as type/relation constraints, so long but logically aligned paths are not unfairly penalized by length bias. This is unusual relative to nearby PRM work because it couples non-compensatory risk propagation with schema distance rather than generic semantic similarity, and it is trained with pairwise ranking on positive/negative paths plus first-error-step supervision. Integrated as SCPRM-MCTS, it guides Monte Carlo Tree Search over medical KGQA, legal KGQA, and CWQ using small base LLMs such as Llama3.1-8B. The result is consistently better Hits@k and more robust behavior under noisy schema parsing, pointing toward future graph-reasoning systems that learn structural safety signals and lookahead rewards instead of only final-answer correctness.

MEMAUDIT: An Exact Package-Oracle Evaluation Protocol for Budgeted Long-Term LLM Memory Writing attacks the evaluation bottleneck in long-horizon agent memory: existing benchmarks score final QA and thereby entangle memory writing with retrieval, prompting, and reader reasoning, so they cannot localize whether a system failed to store the right thing, compress it poorly, or simply retrieve badly later. The paper pivots from end-answer accuracy toward write-time semantic compression under a frozen budget, turning memory evaluation into a finite auditable optimization problem. Its mechanical shift is a MEMAUDIT package that fixes the experience stream, candidate representations, storage costs, evidence units, future-query requirements, and budget, then scores a writer against an exact package oracle with a certified denominator. Instead of treating memory as a generic text buffer, it models one-representation-per-experience selection under a knapsack-plus-partition constraint and uses a concave-over-modular semantic coverage objective, solved exactly by branch-and-bound with MILP certification. What is unusual is the explicit support for validity-state candidates like tombstones and compound updates, so the benchmark can reward preservation of supersession, deletion, and current truth without making the utility nonmonotone. Across exact synthetic packages, validity-heavy stress tests, and audited Natural-87 support slices, MEMAUDIT separates representation quality, invalidation preservation, and budget-aware admission effects that end-to-end QA hides. It also exports Mem0, A-Mem, and Letta stores into a union denominator, distinguishing extraction quality from compaction/selection failure. The direction it points to is a new class of certifiable memory benchmarks for long-term agents that evaluate what is actually preserved under fixed storage budgets.

When Correct Isn't Usable: Improving Structured Output Reliability in Small Language Models attacks a deployment failure mode that standard task benchmarks hide: 7–9B models can solve GSM8K or MATH but still fail to emit parsable JSON with required fields, making “correct” answers unusable downstream. The paper pivots from treating the problem as pure reasoning quality toward structured communication under a hard output contract, challenging the implicit assumption that a good system prompt or grammar-based constrained decoding is enough. Its mechanical shift is to optimize the system prompt itself via AloLab, a black-box iterative loop that queries the target model, deterministically scores both mathematical correctness and JSON validity, then uses a stronger meta-agent (Claude Sonnet 4.5) to rewrite the prompt from observed failure traces and preserve success patterns. This is unusual because it requires no fine-tuning, gradients, demonstrations, or model access, yet directly targets model-specific format pathologies such as markdown-fence wrapping or LaTeX-in-JSON escape failures. Across three open 7–9B models and a GPT-4o probe, naive prompting yields high task accuracy but 0% output accuracy, while AloLab reaches 84–87% output accuracy on GSM8K, 34–40% on MATH, and 95.2% on GPT-4o GSM8K, with near-NAIVE latency; constrained decoding is slower by 3.6×–8.2× and can hurt task quality. The paper points toward prompt-level control planes and black-box reliability optimization as a distinct research direction for structured-output AI systems.

Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers attacks the measurement failure where frontier model “reasoning” is collapsed into pass@1 or exact-match scores on MMLU, GSM8K, MATH, AIME, ARC-AGI, and similar suites, even though those scores cannot tell memorization, comprehension, shortcut exploitation, or contamination-amplified lookup apart from genuine multi-step problem solving. The paper challenges the old benchmarking habit that treats final-answer accuracy as a sufficient proxy and implicitly assumes a single forward pass or latent state is enough to certify reasoning. It pivots from outcome-only leaderboards toward process-based evaluation, defining reasoning as adaptive search: selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions. The core mechanical shift is to make externalized reasoning traces—natural-language chain-of-thought, symbolic scratchpads, proof steps, or tool logs—the evaluation interface, then judge them with trace validity and faithfulness rather than just the final answer. In this framing, transformers and state-space models are structurally limited by fixed-depth parallel computation, so intermediate decoding is not just helpful but the only portable way to expose variable-depth computation. The paper’s distinctive contribution is a researcher guide with evidence tiers, verifier-backed step metrics, intervention-based faithfulness tests, and anytime/halting profiles, plus reporting standards that demand checker interfaces and held-out instance generation. It claims this would make reasoning claims debuggable and less vulnerable to benchmark lottery effects, pointing toward trace-native, checker-backed, contamination-aware AI evaluation and training.

Decoding-Time Debiasing via Process Reward Models: From Controlled Fill-in to Open-Ended Generation attacks the practical failure mode where LLMs reproduce social stereotypes in downstream use, while standard fixes such as RLHF, DPO, or curated retraining are too expensive, require weight access, and can blunt unrelated capabilities. The paper pivots from changing model parameters toward treating fairness as a black-box decoding problem: instead of retraining, it searches over candidate tokens at inference time and uses a separate Process Reward Model to score each candidate for both bias and fluency. The mechanical shift is stepwise, not global: a PRM judges single tokens, then three increasingly structured control loops use that judgment—Best-of-N selection, Sequential critique-and-revise, and Constitutional self-audit—so the generator is steered by token-level fairness feedback rather than prompt-only persuasion. This is unusual because it imports PRM-style process supervision from math reasoning into social bias mitigation, and it extends beyond template fill-in to open-ended generation by debiasing each word on the fly, gated by a lightweight Bias Guard that only triggers on potentially biased words. Evaluated on GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 3 4B, and Qwen 2.5 3B over a 200-prompt English/Urdu benchmark spanning eight bias categories, Sequential gives the strongest and most consistent gains, raising mean bias by up to +0.40 while preserving fluency; Best-of-N often collapses on smaller models, revealing dependence on candidate diversity, and Urdu remains harder. The paper points toward black-box, token-level control systems where fairness, cost, and capability are jointly optimized at decoding time.
attractors.explanation:
Attractor A2 Explanation:

This basin is about multimodal models that must look carefully before they answer. The common thread is active, fine-grained perception: systems that decide where to spend attention or compute, zoom into the right region, and keep uncertain visual evidence in the loop instead of collapsing to a language-only guess. What distinguishes it is that the difficulty is not privacy or model repair, but getting grounded reasoning to survive dense, cluttered inputs and small targets. Papers here converge on search and focus mechanisms for charts, GUIs, and other information-packed scenes, often pairing uncertainty with adaptive routing or latent visual pathways. Compared with adjacent reliability work, the goal is not to forget or defend data, but to extract the right evidence from the image at the moment it matters. Chart-FR1 and AutoFocus fit this pattern: both treat perception as a selective, iterative process rather than a one-shot readout.
nearest_titles: Chart-FR1: Visual Focus-Driven Fine-Grained Reasoning on Dense Charts
AutoFocus: Uncertainty-Aware Active Visual Search for GUI Grounding
Perceptual Flow Network for Visually Grounded Reasoning
Flexi-LoRA with Input-Adaptive Ranks: Efficient Finetuning for Speech and Reasoning Tasks
Visual Latents Know More Than They Say: Unsilencing Latent Reasoning in MLLMs
